one_line_takeaway: For a CERN LHC crystal-collimator alignment CNN, folding the deployed per-window z-normalization and zero-padding plus a channel-correlated noise model into a differentiable wrapper is essential to get honest adversarial-robustness numbers (naive attacks misestimate by up to ~70 points), and PGD adversarial fine-tuning raises robust accuracy by up to 18.6 points without hurting clean accuracy.
key_finding: Omitting any part of the deployed preprocessing pipeline or mis-specifying the threat model can massively distort robustness estimates. On the B=296 test split (clean accuracy 0.939): removing normalization or padding awareness produces 9-23 percentage-point gaps between RA_tool and RA_pipe, and combining a naive threat model with missing preprocessing-awareness yields a ~70 percentage-point gap (Naive no-mask/renorm RA_tool 0.095 under Foolbox vs RA_pipe 0.767). The preprocessing-aware Baseline instead shows RA_tool approximately equal to RA_pipe (0.794/0.794 Foolbox, 0.838/0.838 ART), confirming the optimization problem matches the deployed pipeline. Adversarial FINE-TUNING improves robust accuracy by +4.7 to +18.6 percentage points across radii while even slightly improving clean accuracy (+0.4%, 0.939 -> 0.943), a beneficial data-augmentation effect in a sparse-data regime; however successful attacks still remain, so fully autonomous alignment is judged not yet safe. The proof-of-concept sequence attack shows a single smooth, time-localized perturbation can consistently flip predictions from 'channeling' to 'partial well' across the targeted window range [190,205] while leaving the rest of the scan visually unchanged, demonstrating misclassifications that persist across adjacent windows.
core_contribution: The paper studies and improves the ADVERSARIAL ROBUSTNESS of a deployed CNN that assists crystal-collimator alignment at CERN's LHC by classifying two-channel beam-loss-monitor (BLM) time-series windows into three classes (no channeling / partial well / channeling). Its own contributions are: (1) an operationally-plausible, structured threat model for BLM signals that decomposes each admissible perturbation into a channel-correlated common-mode component plus per-channel independent noise, with separate per-channel L-infinity budgets (eps_com, eps_ind), and that respects zero-padding constraints; (2) Proposition 1, a formal argument that naive L-infinity attacks in the NORMALIZED input space are infeasible/unsound because per-window z-normalization forces mean 0 / std 1 and padding forces zeros, so a normalized-space L-infinity ball contains points that correspond to no real signal; (3) a lightweight differentiable 'reparameterization/wrapper' layer that folds the structured noise model AND the deployed preprocessing (per-window z-normalization + left zero-padding) into the CNN's computation graph, so off-the-shelf gradient attack frameworks (ART, Foolbox) can operate 'preprocessing-aware' on the deployed pipeline without library modification; and (4) an extension of single-window robustness to SEQUENCE-level robustness over full sliding-window scans, defining 'adversarial sequences' (chains of consistent, smooth adversarial examples across consecutive windows) as counterexamples to operator stability requirements, plus a proof-of-concept attack. The wrapper pattern itself is explicitly borrowed from prior work (EOT [1], Semantify-NN [24], DeepCert [27]); the novelty the authors claim is the sensor-consistent common+per-channel decomposition, the preprocessing-aware instantiation for time series, and the window-sequence analysis. The classified CNN and its dataset are NOT this paper's contribution -- they are from Ricci et al. [30].
threat_model: Availability/integrity threat against an automation-assisting classifier in a safety-critical accelerator control loop. Adversary/noise capability is deliberately constrained to physically plausible BLM perturbations: electronic readout and digitization noise (approximately zero-mean iid Gaussian, per channel) plus beam-related fluctuations that are correlated across the crystal-BLM and secondary-BLM channels. Formally, a perturbation delta on window X is admissible iff delta_{t,c} = eps_com_c * u_com_t + eps_ind_c * u_ind_{t,c} with |u|_inf <= 1, is exactly zero on padded indices, and is bounded per channel by eps_com_c + eps_ind_c. The two main operational risk modes are (i) FALSE channeling near a sub-optimal orientation (prematurely stopping the scan, degrading collimation) and (ii) MISSED channeling at the true optimum (unnecessary re-scans, efficiency loss). The paper frames perturbations as realistic sensor/process noise rather than a malicious remote attacker, but treats them as adversarial worst-case within the plausible set.
limitations: (1) Robust-accuracy figures are ATTACK-BASED (PGD), hence non-certified optimistic upper bounds on robustness, not formal guarantees. (2) No complete/formal verification is achieved: the wrapper does not yield a verifier-agnostic encoding when dynamic per-window normalization is included, because variance/sqrt/division need verifier-specific abstract transformers. (3) The adversarial-sequence proof-of-concept maximizes misclassifications but does not guarantee maximality or optimal smoothness, and sequence-level results are a single-trace qualitative demonstration rather than a population-level evaluation. (4) Per-channel budgets (eps_com, eps_ind) are set from empirical BLM noise analysis as a 'conservative upper bound' rather than a rigorously certified physical bound. (5) Evaluation is on one CNN and one dataset (1689 instances, 296-sample test split) from a single CERN case study; transfer to other layouts is argued but not demonstrated. (6) The threat model is framed as plausible sensor/process noise, not an actual capable network/physical attacker, so the 'security' framing is closer to safety-robustness under worst-case noise.

datasets_used: Two-channel (crystal BLM + secondary/absorber BLM) beam-loss-monitor time series from LHC crystal-collimator rotation scans, dataset of 1689 expert-labeled instances from Ricci et al. [30], three classes (no channeling / partial well / channeling)
Test split B=296 from Ricci et al. [30] used for all robust-accuracy measurements
quantitative_results: Clean accuracy of deployed CNN: 0.939 (baseline), 0.943 (adversarially fine-tuned)
Adversarial fine-tuning improves robust accuracy by +4.7% to +18.6% across radii while improving clean accuracy by +0.4%
Table 2 Baseline (full wrapper) RA_tool->RA_pipe: Foolbox 0.794->0.794, ART 0.838->0.838
Table 2 No-normalization: Foolbox 0.733->0.831, ART 0.777->0.868; No-padding: Foolbox 0.669->0.899, ART 0.716->0.919 (9-23 pp RA_tool/RA_pipe gaps)
Table 2 Naive (mask+renorm): Foolbox 0.669->0.669, ART 0.713->0.713; Naive (no mask/renorm): Foolbox 0.095->0.767, ART 0.152->0.818 (~70 pp gap vs baseline threat model)
Main-attack per-channel budgets (eps_com, eps_ind) = (0.10, 0.02); attack = untargeted L-infinity PGD at radius eps=1 on structured budget
Proof-of-concept sequence attack targets windows [190,205], smoothly flipping 'channeling' -> 'partial well'
CNN input shape (630,2); architecture Conv1D 256ch -> Conv1D 160ch -> Global Average Pooling -> Dense(3)
baselines_compared: Preprocessing-aware Baseline wrapper vs No-normalization (frozen mu,sigma) vs No-padding vs Naive(mask+renorm) vs Naive(no mask/renorm) ablation configurations
Structured common+per-channel threat model vs global L-infinity ball in signal space vs global L-infinity ball in normalized space (A2)
Baseline CNN vs adversarially fine-tuned CNN (defense evaluation)
Two attack frameworks compared head-to-head: ART [25] and Foolbox [28] (both PGD backends)
Two robustness metrics contrasted: tool-reported RA_tool vs pipeline-checked RA_pipe
